ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Thai vegetarian mixed vegetables with mushrooms. FOODON_03311737 (stir-fried vegetable dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP for stir-fried vegetables is the closest parent. No specific code exists for Thai vegetarian mixed vegetables with mushrooms.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Thai vegetarian stir-fried vegetable dish.

main_ingredients: mixed vegetables (cabbage, carrot, broccoli, baby corn, snap peas)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Thai vegetarian mixed vegetables with mushrooms is approximately 250 g, typically served as a side dish or main vegetable course with steamed rice.

nutrient_source.title: Mixed Vegetables Stir-Fried — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table composite estimate
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Thai vegetarian mixed vegetables with mushrooms exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for stir-fried mixed vegetables (NDB 11998, 11819, 11260) and mushrooms (NDB 11260), weighted by typical Thai restaurant proportions. FAO/INFOODS SEA table values for stir-fried vegetable dishes were used as a cross-check. Sodium reflects typical Thai restaurant seasoning with vegetarian oyster sauce and soy sauce.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — mixed non-starchy vegetables and mushroom-containing vegetable dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai vegetarian mixed vegetables with mushrooms. GI of 35 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for mixed non-starchy vegetable dishes (GI range 30–40) and mushrooms (GI approximately 28–38). Mushrooms and non-starchy vegetables are consistently low-GI foods. GL per serving calculated as (35 × 6.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 250 g serving) / 100 = 5.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce and vegetarian oyster sauce
hypertension.guidance: A 250 g serving provides approximately 950 mg sodium, which represents about 41% of the 2300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced-sauce preparation or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high dietary fibre from non-starchy vegetables and mushrooms
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 35 and 2.2 g dietary fibre per 100 g, this dish produces a low glycaemic load of approximately 5.7 per serving, making it a suitable vegetable dish for blood glucose management in type 2 diabetes when consumed with a balanced meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and high vegetable fibre content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish contains only 0.5 g saturated fat and 0 mg cholesterol per 100 g, with beneficial dietary fibre and potassium from vegetables; however, the moderate sodium content warrants attention for patients with heart failure or sodium-sensitive cardiovascular conditions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetarian Mixed Vegetables with Mushrooms is a Thai stir-fried vegetable dish consisting of a colourful assortment of non-starchy vegetables such as cabbage, carrot, broccoli, baby corn, and snap peas, combined with mushrooms (typically oyster or shiitake mushrooms) and seasoned with vegetarian oyster sauce, soy sauce, garlic, and a small amount of sesame oil. The dish is cooked rapidly in a hot wok over high heat, preserving the crisp texture and vibrant colour of the vegetables while developing a savoury, lightly umami-flavoured sauce. In Thai cuisine, this style of stir-fried mixed vegetables (phak ruam phat) is a staple side dish served alongside steamed jasmine rice or as part of a shared Thai meal. The mushrooms contribute a meaty, earthy texture that makes the dish satisfying despite being fully vegetarian. Nutritionally, the dish is low in calories (approximately 62 kcal per 100 g), low in fat, and provides useful amounts of dietary fibre (2.2 g per 100 g), vitamin A, vitamin C, potassium, and iron from the diverse vegetable mix. The glycaemic index is low (approximately 35), making it appropriate for individuals managing blood glucose levels. The primary nutritional concern is sodium, as the soy sauce and vegetarian oyster sauce contribute approximately 380 mg sodium per 100 g. This dish is suitable for vegetarians and can be made vegan by confirming the oyster sauce is plant-based. It is commonly available at Thai restaurants in Singapore, including casual dining establishments.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for this specific Thai restaurant dish was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA tables. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for component vegetables and mushrooms, weighted by typical Thai restaurant proportions, and cross-checked against FAO/INFOODS SEA stir-fried vegetable dish values. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for mixed non-starchy vegetable dishes. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component ingredients are well-characterised in approved sources and the estimation methodology is transparent.
notes: Dish name in input record was '610D Vegetarian Mixed Vegetable w Mushro' — this is a menu item code and truncated name from field survey data (Sanook Kitchen, Funan). Canonical English name normalised to 'Vegetarian Mixed Vegetables with Mushrooms'. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy or FAO/INFOODS SEA source data for this vegetable dish category. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the source data for this dish type. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 as this is a fully plant-based dish containing no animal products; confirmed zero for plant-based vegetable dishes per USDA FoodData Central. Trans fat is 0.0 as no hydrogenated oils are used in standard Thai vegetarian stir-fry preparation; confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy vegetable dish entries. Cholesterol is 0.0 as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; naturally zero for plant foods. The vegan flag is set to false because standard vegetarian oyster sauce may contain trace animal-derived ingredients depending on brand; consumers should verify with the restaurant. Sodium value reflects typical Thai restaurant seasoning levels and may vary significantly between establishments.

improvement_actions_taken: Normalised truncated menu item name '610D Vegetarian Mixed Vegetable w Mushro' to canonical English name 'Vegetarian Mixed Vegetables with Mushrooms'.
Added Thai native-script local name and alias as required by G6 multilingual rule for Thai cuisine.
Added Chinese simplified and traditional names for Singapore restaurant context.
Estimated nutrient values from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and FAO/INFOODS SEA with transparent methodology documentation.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, selenium_mcg) with explicit source-based justification in notes.
Documented all 0.0 values (trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg) with confirmed-zero or naturally-zero justifications in notes.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases are perfectly consistent before finalising.
Used only approved sources: USDA FoodData Central, FAO/INFOODS SEA, University of Sydney GI Database.
